topics.title: 1. Introduction to Web Application Security
topics.definition: Web application security is the process of protecting websites, web applications, APIs, databases, users, and backend systems from unauthorized access, data theft, manipulation, service disruption, and malicious code execution.
examPoints: A web application generally consists of:
A vulnerability in any of these components can compromise the entire application.
comparisons: Objective: Meaning
Confidentiality: Data must be accessible only to authorized users
Integrity: Data must not be modified without authorization
Availability: Applications and services must remain accessible
Authentication: Verify the identity of a user
Authorization: Determine what an authenticated user can access
Accountability: Record and trace user and system actions
Non-repudiation: Prevent a user from denying an action performed

topics.title: 2. OWASP Top 10 – 2021
topics.definition: OWASP stands for the Open Worldwide Application Security Project. The OWASP Top 10 identifies major security risks affecting web applications.

topics.title: A01: Broken Access Control
topics.definition: Broken access control occurs when an application fails to properly restrict what authenticated or unauthenticated users can access or modify.
examPoints: A normal user accesses an administrator page.
A user changes user_id=101 to user_id=102 and sees another user's data.
A user modifies role=user to role=admin.
A user accesses restricted URLs directly.

topics.title: A02: Cryptographic Failures
topics.definition: Cryptographic failures occur when sensitive data is exposed because encryption is missing, weak, incorrectly implemented, or poorly managed.
examPoints: Using weak hashes such as MD5 or SHA-1
Store passwords using password-hashing algorithms such as Argon2, bcrypt, scrypt, or PBKDF2.
Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit.

topics.title: A03: Injection
topics.definition: Injection occurs when untrusted input is interpreted as part of a command or query.
examPoints: The condition becomes true and may bypass authentication.
Apply least privilege to database accounts.

topics.title: A04: Insecure Design
topics.definition: Insecure design refers to security weaknesses introduced during application architecture and design.

topics.title: A05: Security Misconfiguration
topics.definition: Security misconfiguration occurs when systems are deployed with insecure settings.

topics.title: A06: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
topics.definition: Applications become vulnerable when they use unsupported or outdated libraries, frameworks, plugins, operating systems, or servers.
examPoints: Old versions of Apache or PHP

topics.title: A07: Identification and Authentication Failures
topics.definition: These failures allow attackers to compromise user accounts or sessions.

topics.title: A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures
topics.definition: These failures occur when software, updates, or data are trusted without integrity verification.

topics.title: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
topics.definition: An organization may fail to detect or respond to attacks if important security events are not logged or monitored.
examPoints: No alerts are generated for suspicious activity.

topics.title: A10: Server-Side Request Forgery
topics.definition: SSRF occurs when an application fetches a URL supplied by a user without proper validation.
examPoints: The vulnerable server sends the request to an internal service.

topics.title: 3. Injection Vulnerabilities
topics.definition: Injection vulnerabilities occur when an application sends untrusted data to an interpreter.

topics.title: SQL Injection
topics.definition: SQL Injection manipulates database queries through malicious input.
examPoints: Uses the UNION operator to combine query results.
The attacker causes database errors that reveal information such as:
The application does not display database data, but the attacker infers information from different responses.
The attacker infers results using response delays.
Web application firewall as an additional control

topics.title: Command Injection
topics.definition: Command injection occurs when user input is passed to an operating-system shell.
examPoints: Malicious input may append another operating-system command.

topics.title: LDAP Injection
topics.definition: LDAP injection manipulates LDAP filters used for directory authentication or searches.

topics.title: NoSQL Injection
topics.definition: NoSQL injection targets databases such as MongoDB.
examPoints: This may cause a query to match more records than intended.
Reject query operators from user input.
Avoid directly using client-supplied JSON in queries.

topics.title: 4. Inclusion Vulnerabilities
topics.definition: Inclusion vulnerabilities occur when user input controls which file an application loads.

topics.title: Local File Inclusion
topics.definition: LFI loads files from the local server.

topics.title: Remote File Inclusion
topics.definition: RFI loads a file from a remote server.
examPoints: Do not accept arbitrary file paths.
Use an allowlist of approved files.

topics.title: 5. Cross-Site Scripting
topics.definition: XSS occurs when attacker-controlled script executes in another user's browser under the context of a trusted website.

topics.title: Stored XSS
topics.definition: The malicious script is permanently stored on the server.
examPoints: Every user who views the affected page may execute the script.

topics.title: Reflected XSS
topics.definition: The malicious input is immediately returned in the server's response.
examPoints: The victim normally needs to click a crafted link.

topics.title: DOM-Based XSS
topics.definition: The vulnerability exists in client-side JavaScript. The server may not process the malicious input.

topics.title: XSS Mitigation
topics.definition: HttpOnly helps prevent JavaScript from reading a cookie.

topics.title: 6. Injection in Stored Procedures
topics.definition: Stored procedures are not automatically safe from SQL Injection.
examPoints: They become vulnerable when they dynamically construct SQL statements using string concatenation.

topics.title: Vulnerable stored procedure
topics.definition: The procedure remains vulnerable because user input becomes part of the SQL command.

topics.title: Secure stored procedure
topics.definition: # Session 2
examPoints: Use sp_executesql with parameters when dynamic SQL is unavoidable.

topics.title: 7. Denial of Service
topics.definition: A DoS attack attempts to make a system or service unavailable to legitimate users.

topics.title: DoS vs DDoS
comparisons: DoS: DDoS
Usually originates from one source: Originates from multiple systems
Easier to identify and block: More difficult to block
Limited attack capacity: Uses distributed attack capacity
May use a single attacking machine: Commonly uses a botnet

topics.title: Types of DoS attacks
topics.definition: The attacker overwhelms network bandwidth.
examPoints: The attacker sends expensive application requests.

topics.title: Mitigation
topics.definition: All DoS testing must be performed only in an authorized laboratory environment.

topics.title: 8. Buffer Overflow
topics.definition: A buffer overflow occurs when a program writes more data into a memory buffer than the buffer can hold.
examPoints: gets() does not check input length.

topics.title: 9. Input Validation
topics.definition: Input validation ensures that supplied data matches expected type, format, size, range, and structure.

topics.title: Types of validation
topics.definition: Accept only known valid input.
examPoints: Ensure that a numeric field receives a number.
Ensure a value falls within a permitted range.
Ensure the input is logically permitted.
A withdrawal amount must not exceed the account balance.

topics.title: Client-side vs server-side validation
comparisons: Client-side validation: Server-side validation
Runs in the browser: Runs on the server
Improves user experience: Enforces security
Can be modified or bypassed: Cannot be directly bypassed by changing browser code
Must not be trusted alone: Mandatory

topics.title: Validation vs sanitization vs encoding
comparisons: Technique: Purpose
Validation: Confirms that input is acceptable
Sanitization: Removes or neutralizes unwanted content
Encoding: Converts special characters so they are treated as data
Parameterization: Separates commands from user-controlled data

topics.title: 10. Access Control
topics.definition: Access control determines who can access a resource and what actions they may perform.

topics.title: Authentication vs authorization
comparisons: Authentication: Authorization
Verifies identity: Determines permissions
“Who are you?”: “What are you allowed to do?”
Happens before authorization: Happens after authentication

topics.title: Access-control models
topics.definition: The resource owner decides permissions.
examPoints: A central policy controls access based on security labels.
Access depends on attributes such as:

topics.title: Access-control principles
topics.definition: # Session 3

topics.title: 11. Web Application Security Risks
topics.definition: Common risks include:

topics.title: 12. Threat Risk Modelling
topics.definition: Threat modelling is a structured process for identifying assets, threats, vulnerabilities, attack paths, and security controls.

topics.title: Risk calculation
topics.definition: A common simplified model is:

topics.title: STRIDE model
comparisons: Letter: Threat
S: Spoofing
T: Tampering
R: Repudiation
I: Information Disclosure
D: Denial of Service
E: Elevation of Privilege

topics.title: Authorization
topics.definition: Carries authentication credentials or tokens.
examPoints: Sensitive authorization headers must not be logged carelessly.

topics.title: Host
topics.definition: Identifies the requested hostname.
examPoints: Improper Host-header handling may lead to:

topics.title: Referer
topics.definition: Indicates the page that initiated a request.
examPoints: It may leak sensitive data if confidential information appears in URLs.

topics.title: Origin
topics.definition: Identifies the origin of a cross-origin request.
examPoints: Used in CORS and CSRF-related security checks.

topics.title: Content-Type
topics.definition: Specifies the request or response media type.
examPoints: Improper content-type handling may contribute to parser confusion.

topics.title: Content-Security-Policy
topics.definition: Restricts the sources from which scripts and other resources may load.

topics.title: X-Frame-Options
topics.definition: Helps prevent clickjacking.

topics.title: Strict-Transport-Security
topics.definition: Forces browsers to use HTTPS.

topics.title: Set-Cookie
topics.definition: Creates or updates cookies.

topics.title: Cache-Control
topics.definition: Prevents caching of sensitive content.

topics.title: X-Content-Type-Options
topics.definition: Prevents MIME-type sniffing.

topics.title: 14. Burp Suite
topics.definition: Burp Suite is an integrated web-application security testing platform.

topics.title: Proxy
topics.definition: Intercepts browser requests and server responses.

topics.title: Repeater
topics.definition: Used to manually modify and resend individual requests.

topics.title: Intruder
topics.definition: Automates repeated requests using payloads.
examPoints: Intruder must only be used against authorized systems.

topics.title: Scanner
topics.definition: Automatically identifies common web vulnerabilities.
examPoints: Scanner findings must be manually verified because automated scanners can produce false positives and false negatives.

topics.title: Decoder
topics.definition: Encodes and decodes data.

topics.title: Comparer
topics.definition: Compares two requests or responses to identify differences.

topics.title: Target
topics.definition: Maps the application's:

topics.title: Extender
topics.definition: Adds extensions to Burp Suite.

topics.title: 15. Data Extraction
topics.definition: Data extraction means obtaining sensitive information from a vulnerable application.

topics.title: 16. Advanced Identification and Exploitation
topics.definition: Advanced vulnerability identification goes beyond automated scanning.

topics.title: Vulnerability chaining
topics.definition: Attackers may combine several low- or medium-risk vulnerabilities.

topics.title: GET
topics.definition: Retrieves a resource.
examPoints: Data commonly appears in the URL
GET should not perform sensitive state-changing operations.

topics.title: POST
topics.definition: Submits data or creates a resource.

topics.title: PUT
topics.definition: Creates or replaces a resource at a specified location.

topics.title: PATCH
topics.definition: Partially updates a resource.

topics.title: DELETE
topics.definition: Deletes a resource.

topics.title: HEAD
topics.definition: Returns headers without the response body.

topics.title: OPTIONS
topics.definition: Returns supported methods or communication options.

topics.title: TRACE
topics.definition: Echoes a request back to the client.
examPoints: It is often disabled because it may expose sensitive request information.

topics.title: Mitigation
examPoints: Apply authentication and authorization to every method.

topics.title: 18. Wireshark for Web Analysis
topics.definition: Wireshark captures and analyzes network packets.

topics.title: Capture filter
topics.definition: A capture filter decides which packets are collected.

topics.title: Display filter
topics.definition: A display filter decides which captured packets are shown.

topics.title: What Wireshark can reveal
topics.definition: HTTPS traffic can normally be captured, but its application payload is encrypted.

topics.title: 19. SAST
topics.definition: SAST stands for Static Application Security Testing.
examPoints: It analyzes application source code, bytecode, or binaries without executing the application.

topics.title: Limitations
examPoints: May struggle with complex business logic

topics.title: 20. DAST
topics.definition: DAST stands for Dynamic Application Security Testing.
examPoints: It tests a running application from an external perspective.

topics.title: Advantages
examPoints: Does not always require source code

topics.title: Limitations
examPoints: Finds vulnerabilities later in the lifecycle
May not identify the exact vulnerable code line
May produce false positives and false negatives

topics.title: SAST vs DAST
topics.definition: Using both provides better coverage.
comparisons: SAST: DAST
Tests source code or binaries: Tests a running application
White-box approach: Black-box approach
Used early in development: Used during testing or deployment
Identifies vulnerable code locations: Identifies exploitable runtime behaviour
Detects insecure coding patterns: Detects runtime and configuration issues
Examples: Checkmarx, Fortify: Examples: ZAP, Burp Suite

topics.title: 21. Web Application Framework Security
topics.definition: Frameworks simplify development but may introduce risk when misconfigured.

topics.title: Django example
topics.definition: Important controls include:
examPoints: Framework security features are effective only when correctly configured.

topics.title: 22. Browser JSGuard
topics.definition: JSGuard is a browser-security extension intended to identify suspicious or malicious JavaScript behaviour.

topics.title: Limitation
topics.definition: A browser extension is an additional defensive layer. It does not replace:

topics.title: CVE
topics.definition: CVE stands for Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures.
examPoints: A CVE identifier gives a standardized name to a publicly known vulnerability.

topics.title: NVD
topics.definition: NVD stands for National Vulnerability Database.
examPoints: It enriches CVE records with information such as:

topics.title: CVE vs CVSS
topics.definition: A high CVSS score does not automatically mean the highest business risk. Asset importance and exposure must also be considered.
comparisons: CVE: CVSS
Identifies a vulnerability: Measures severity
Standard naming system: Scoring system
Does not itself represent risk: Helps prioritize remediation

topics.title: 24. OWASP Web Security Testing Guide
topics.definition: The OWASP Web Security Testing Guide provides a structured methodology for testing web applications.

topics.title: 25. Practical Lab Workflow
topics.definition: A safe web-application security lab should use intentionally vulnerable platforms such as:

topics.title: Suggested workflow
topics.definition: Testing must be restricted to systems explicitly authorized for assessment.

topics.title: Vulnerability vs threat vs risk
comparisons: Term: Meaning
Asset: Something valuable
Threat: Something capable of causing harm
Vulnerability: A weakness
Exploit: A method that takes advantage of a vulnerability
Risk: Likelihood and impact of harm
Control: A safeguard that reduces risk

topics.title: SQL Injection vs XSS
comparisons: SQL Injection: XSS
Targets database queries: Targets browser execution
Executes database commands: Executes JavaScript or HTML
Often affects server-side data: Primarily affects users
Prevented by parameterization: Prevented by output encoding and sanitization

topics.title: LFI vs RFI
comparisons: LFI: RFI
Includes local server files: Includes remote files
Can expose /etc/passwd: Can load attacker-hosted code
Primarily path manipulation: Primarily remote URL inclusion

topics.title: Reflected vs Stored vs DOM XSS
comparisons: Type: Location
Reflected XSS: Payload returned in immediate server response
Stored XSS: Payload stored on the server
DOM XSS: Payload processed by client-side JavaScript

topics.title: IDOR vs SQL Injection
comparisons: IDOR: SQL Injection
Authorization failure: Injection failure
Manipulates object identifiers: Manipulates SQL query structure
Example: changing invoice ID: Example: ' OR '1'='1

topics.title: Burp Proxy vs Repeater vs Intruder vs Scanner
comparisons: Tool: Purpose
Proxy: Intercept and modify traffic
Repeater: Manually resend requests
Intruder: Automate payload-based testing
Scanner: Automatically identify vulnerabilities

topics.title: 27. Important Exam Points
oneLiners: OWASP Top 10 is a risk-awareness document, not a complete testing methodology.
Input validation alone does not prevent every vulnerability.
Parameterized queries are the main defence against SQL Injection.
Stored procedures can still be vulnerable when they use dynamic SQL.
Output encoding is essential for preventing XSS.
Client-side validation can be bypassed.
Authorization must be enforced on the server.
HTTPS protects data in transit but does not fix insecure application logic.
SAST examines code without running the application.
DAST tests a running application.
Burp Repeater is used for manual request modification.
Burp Intruder automates repeated payload testing.
Wireshark captures packets but does not automatically prove a web vulnerability.
Risk is commonly prioritized using likelihood and impact.
CVE identifies a vulnerability; CVSS scores its severity.
LFI includes local files; RFI includes remote files.
Stored XSS persists on the server.
Reflected XSS normally requires a crafted request or link.
DOM XSS occurs through unsafe client-side JavaScript.
DoS primarily attacks availability.
Least privilege limits the impact of compromise.
Security headers reduce risk but do not replace secure coding.
Automated scanner findings must be manually verified.
HTTP methods must be individually authorized.
Testing must be conducted only in an authorized environment.

topics.title: 28. Quick Revision Sheet
workflows: OWASP A06 → Vulnerable and Outdated Components
OWASP A07 → Identification and Authentication Failures
OWASP A08 → Software and Data Integrity Failures
OWASP A09 → Logging and Monitoring Failures
DoS Defence → Rate limiting and traffic filtering
Burp Repeater → Modify and resend manually
